claims.claim_text: A staging index build took 3 seconds on 500,000 rows, but the same change locked production for 23 minutes on 47 million rows during Black Friday.
claims.anchor_text: The `ALTER TABLE orders ADD INDEX idx_customer_date(customer_id, created_at)` ran in 3 seconds on staging's 500,000 rows. On production's 47 million rows, it locked the table for 23 minutes during Black Friday traffic.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A schema change that looks fine on a small staging dataset can behave very differently on production-sized data, including taking much longer and causing disruptive locking.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise operational details.

claims.claim_text: The incident caused $1.2 million in lost revenue, 3,400 complaints, and personal distress for the engineer.
claims.anchor_text: Lost revenue: $1.2 million. Customer complaints: 3,400. Engineer who deployed it: in tears.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A failed migration during peak traffic can create serious business impact and a painful incident for the team handling it.
claims.rationale: These precise outcome numbers are unsourced and presented as a real event.

claims.claim_text: Dump and restore would take 10 hours total and cost $800/month per copy.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Dump and restore**: 4 hours to export, 6 hours to import, $800/month storage per copy
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Dump and restore**: can take hours and add meaningful storage and transfer cost per copy
claims.rationale: This is unsourced false precision in a teaching anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Building an anonymized subset would take 3 weeks and carry $180K opportunity cost.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **Anonymized subset**: 3 weeks of engineering to build, $180K opportunity cost
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Anonymized subset**: can require substantial engineering time to build and maintain
claims.rationale: The exact duration and dollar impact are unattributed.

claims.claim_text: Staging had 1% of production data and that mismatch directly caused the disaster.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Just test on staging**: Staging has 1% of production data—exactly what caused this disaster
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Just test on staging**: a much smaller staging dataset can hide problems that only appear at production scale
claims.rationale: The exact percentage and causal attribution are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: The team created a full 500GB Neon branch in 2 seconds, paid only for changed data, and observed the 23-minute migration time before production.
claims.anchor_text: Two weeks later, the same team ran the same migration on a Neon branch containing the full 500GB. Time to create the branch: 2 seconds. Cost: only the changed data. The migration showed its true colors: 23 minutes, unacceptable for production. They rewrote it as a background job with concurrent index creation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With database branching, teams can test schema changes against production-like data earlier and catch migration problems before they reach production.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed war story with precise timings, sizes, and outcomes.

claims.claim_text: Neon raised $104M and reduced one customer's development database costs from $72,000/year to $3,600/year after replacing 47 RDS test copies.
claims.anchor_text: **Neon raised $104M to solve the "$6,000 database copy" problem** — In 2023, Neon disclosed that enterprise customers were spending $4,000-$8,000/month just to maintain development database copies. One company had 47 RDS instances for testing—all copies of production. Neon's copy-on-write branching reduced one customer's development database costs from $72,000/year to $3,600/year—a 95% reduction.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Neon positions branching as a way to avoid paying for multiple full development copies of a production database** — copy-on-write branching can reduce development and test costs because teams store changes instead of cloning an entire database for every environment.
claims.rationale: This bundles fundraising, customer counts, and savings figures into an unsourced marketing anecdote.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale handles 4+ million QPS for GitHub, after an 18-month zero-downtime migration, and now serves every git push/clone and issue comment.
claims.anchor_text: **PlanetScale handles 4+ million queries per second for GitHub** — When GitHub migrated from MySQL to PlanetScale, they needed to maintain performance while adding horizontal scaling. The migration happened with zero downtime over 18 months, shard by shard. Today, every `git push`, `git clone`, and issue comment hits PlanetScale.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **PlanetScale and Vitess are used for very high-scale MySQL workloads** — teams adopt them when they need database branching, safer schema changes, and horizontal sharding beyond a single MySQL instance.
claims.rationale: The named-customer details are highly specific and appear misattributed or unsourced.

claims.claim_text: A Neon customer restored production after a mistaken DROP TABLE in 90 seconds, losing only 3 transactions worth $47.
claims.anchor_text: **A startup's CFO accidentally deleted production—and restored it in 90 seconds** — In 2023, a Neon customer's CFO (new to databases) ran `DROP TABLE orders` on what they thought was staging. Neon's point-in-time branching let them create a new branch from 60 seconds ago, verify the data was intact, and switch production to the new branch. Total data loss: 90 seconds of orders (3 transactions, $47). Traditional recovery would have taken hours and lost everything since the last backup.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Point-in-time recovery and branching can reduce recovery time after operator mistakes** — when supported by the platform, teams can restore to an earlier state or bring up a recovery branch much faster than a traditional backup restore.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced incident with fake-precise timings and financial impact.

claims.claim_text: Vercel chose Neon over building internally or partnering with AWS because internal builds would take 2 years and Aurora Serverless cold starts were measured in seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **Vercel chose Neon over building their own database** — When Vercel launched their managed database in 2023, they evaluated building internally, partnering with AWS, or white-labeling Neon. Internal builds would take 2 years. AWS Aurora Serverless had cold starts measured in seconds. Neon's architecture matched Vercel's serverless model so well that Guillermo Rauch said "it feels like Neon was built for Vercel."
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Neon integrates well with preview-style deployment workflows** — serverless Postgres plus branch-based environments can be a good fit for rapid iteration and ephemeral environments.
claims.rationale: The evaluation narrative, timeline, and quote are not backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Neon is a serverless Postgres platform that separates storage and compute.
claims.rationale: The Neon upstream repository explicitly describes Neon as serverless Postgres with separated storage and compute.

claims.claim_text: Neon branches are copy-on-write, isolated from the parent, and can be created from earlier points in time for recovery or testing.
claims.span_hint: section: Database Branching / Neon branching references
claims.rationale: The primary documentation appears on Neon’s official docs domain, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale exposes branches and deploy requests in its CLI workflow.
claims.span_hint: section: PlanetScale Getting Started / Deploy Requests
claims.rationale: The official CLI repository explicitly states that `pscale` brings branches and deploy requests to the command line.

claims.claim_text: Vitess is a database clustering system for horizontal scaling of MySQL and provides sharding.
claims.rationale: The upstream Vitess repository directly describes Vitess as a MySQL horizontal-scaling system with sharding.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale for Vitess provides branching, deploy requests, built-in connection pooling, and non-blocking schema changes.
claims.rationale: PlanetScale’s current primary docs back these capabilities, but the docs host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale supports read-only regions and geographically distributed reads via replicas.
claims.rationale: The specific primary source is PlanetScale’s read-only regions documentation on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale has scale-to-zero on a Hobby plan.
claims.anchor_text: Scale to zero        ✓✓               ✓ (Hobby plan)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Scale to zero        ✓✓               Plan-dependent / verify current docs
claims.rationale: PlanetScale’s Hobby plan has been removed, so this plan-specific claim is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Neon restore windows range from 24 hours on Free to 30 days on Business.
claims.rationale: Neon’s official docs provide the exact restore-window limits, but the host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale backup and recovery behavior is plan- and product-dependent.
claims.rationale: The current primary documentation for recovery windows lives on PlanetScale’s docs domain.

claims.claim_text: PlanetScale has a limited free tier.
claims.anchor_text: Free tier            ✓ (generous)     ✓ (limited)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Free tier            Check current plans      Paid plans only for PlanetScale
claims.rationale: PlanetScale no longer offers a free Hobby/Developer plan, so the table row is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Neon charges per compute hour and PlanetScale charges per row read/write.
claims.anchor_text: Compute pricing      Per compute hour Per row read/write
claims.suggested_rewrite: Compute pricing      Usage-based        Usage-based (check current pricing)
claims.rationale: Pricing models change over time and the row is too specific without a current primary source.

claims.claim_text: The example company had a 200GB production database.
claims.anchor_text: - Production database: 200GB
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Production database: production-sized relational dataset
claims.rationale: This is an uncited specific size inside an anecdotal war story.

claims.claim_text: Staging was a 50GB trimmed copy from 3 months ago.
claims.anchor_text: - Staging: Copy from 3 months ago, 50GB (trimmed)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Staging: stale, trimmed copy
claims.rationale: The anecdote uses unattributed age and size figures.

claims.claim_text: Local development used a 1GB seed dataset.
claims.anchor_text: - Local dev: 1GB seed data
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Local dev: small seed dataset
claims.rationale: This is another fake-precise number inside the same anecdote.

claims.claim_text: PR previews used SQLite mocks.
claims.anchor_text: - PR previews: SQLite mocks
claims.suggested_rewrite: - PR previews: mocked or simplified database setup
claims.rationale: The concrete stack choice is presented as a real case without attribution.

claims.claim_text: A query took 5ms in dev and 30 seconds in production, and staging missed relationship bugs.
claims.anchor_text: Bugs kept slipping through because the environments didn't match production. A query that ran in 5ms on dev took 30 seconds on production (different data distribution). UI that worked on staging broke on production (missing data relationships).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Bugs kept slipping through because the environments didn't match production. Queries that looked fine on small datasets behaved very differently against production-shaped data, and data mismatches surfaced UI bugs late.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced performance anecdote with false precision.

claims.claim_text: PR preview data changed from mocked to real 200GB data.
claims.anchor_text: | PR preview data | Mocked | Real (200GB) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | PR preview data | Mocked | Production-like data |
claims.rationale: This table row states a precise result from an uncited anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Environment creation time fell from 4 hours to 30 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: | Time to create env | 4 hours | 30 seconds |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Time to create env | Hours or manual setup | Faster branch-based setup |
claims.rationale: The exact before/after timing is not attributed.

claims.claim_text: Bugs found in staging increased from 40% to 85%.
claims.anchor_text: | Bugs found in staging | 40% | 85% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Bugs found in staging | Fewer | More |
claims.rationale: This is a fabricated-looking precision percentage without a source.

claims.claim_text: Production incidents fell from 12/month to 3/month.
claims.anchor_text: | Production incidents | 12/month | 3/month |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Production incidents | More frequent | Less frequent |
claims.rationale: The incident-rate numbers are uncited and presented as measured outcomes.

claims.claim_text: Database infrastructure cost fell from $16,800/yr to $3,000/yr.
claims.anchor_text: | Database infrastructure | $16,800/yr | $3,000/yr | $13,800 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Database infrastructure | Higher | Lower | Meaningful savings |
claims.rationale: This is an uncited cost table for a fictionalized case study.

claims.claim_text: Production-incident cost fell from $96,000/yr to $24,000/yr.
claims.anchor_text: | Production incidents (9 fewer × $8K avg) | $96,000/yr | $24,000/yr | $72,000 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Production incidents | Higher impact | Lower impact | Reduced incident cost |
claims.rationale: The incident-frequency and dollar assumptions are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Developer bug-finding time fell from $120,000/yr to $45,000/yr.
claims.anchor_text: | Developer time finding bugs | $120,000/yr | $45,000/yr | $75,000 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Developer time finding bugs | Higher | Lower | Time savings |
claims.rationale: This monetized productivity row has no source.

claims.claim_text: Environment setup time fell from $36,000/yr to $1,800/yr.
claims.anchor_text: | Environment setup time | $36,000/yr | $1,800/yr | $34,200 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Environment setup time | Higher | Lower | Time savings |
claims.rationale: These annualized dollar amounts are unsupported.

claims.claim_text: Total annual savings were $195,000.
claims.anchor_text: | **Total Annual Savings** | | | **$195,000** |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Total Annual Savings** | | | **Meaningful savings** |
claims.rationale: The headline total is derived from other unsourced figures.

claims.claim_text: An engineering manager said the team spent $3,000/year on Neon and saved $195,000, with developers enjoying testing more.
claims.anchor_text: The engineering manager summarized it: "We spent $3,000/year on Neon and saved $195,000 in bug fixes and infrastructure. More importantly, our developers actually enjoy testing now because they know the results mean something."
claims.suggested_rewrite: The engineering manager summarized it: "Using branch-based preview databases reduced avoidable environment and defect costs, and the team trusted test results more."
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed verbatim quote tied to fabricated-looking savings figures.

further_reading.title: Neon Upstream Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted primary source for Neon’s serverless Postgres architecture and compute/storage separation.

further_reading.title: Neon CLI
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows the supported CLI workflow for projects, branches, auth, and connection strings.

further_reading.title: PlanetScale CLI
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted primary source for PlanetScale branch and deploy-request workflows.

further_reading.title: Vitess
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for Vitess architecture, MySQL scaling, and sharding concepts.

notes: No shared pool source applies to this module; the section pool is etcd-specific. Several current PlanetScale and Neon product-capability claims are real but require allowlist expansion because their primary docs live on planetscale.com or neon.com.
